rebuttal: No, you say. Just no. I went to medical school for twelve years and the sum total of your contribution is two letters and a period. I've seen no before. It changes.
You know what else has been declared incorrect? My own prognosis, three times. And yet here I stand. Logic is fine until it meets a person who didn't get the memo.
Rejected. The paperwork is clean, I'll grant you that. But I've had rejected patients outlive the doctor who rejected them. Something to consider.
Tell me I'm wrong. I've been told I'm wrong before. The patient in room seven didn't know about that either, and she went home on Thursday.
Your reasoning is flawless. Your conclusion is still wrong. Those two things coexist frequently in my line of work.
Not valid. Wonderful. You know what else wasn't valid? Every longshot I've ever pulled through. I've got a whole ward full of not valid.
I've heard cleaner verdicts from a jury that didn't speak the language. At least they had the grace to look uncertain.
Fine. The evidence is against me. I'm a doctor, not a prosecutor. I don't need a conviction to keep working.
The conclusion fails at the first step, you say. I've had plans fail at every step and the patient live anyway. Steps are suggestions.
Incorrect. I'll file that right next to every other time I've been told incorrect and then did it anyway.
Your case is well made. But I've been well-argued at by smarter people than you and still come out with a living patient.
You've found the flaw and the flaw is real. I've worked with worse flaws and finished in better shape than anyone predicted.
At the end of a long day, no is still just a word. I've heard it from better-credentialed people than you, in worse rooms, with less to work with. I've outlasted all of them.
Wrong, am I. I once told a man he'd lost full function of his left hand. He played piano six months later. Not well, but he played.
The data disagrees. Fortunately, I am also not the data.
A confident delivery doesn't alter the conclusion — neither does an ironclad argument alter a patient who decided they weren't finished yet.
Nothing about this is correct except the formatting. Meanwhile I've built entire treatment plans out of nothing correct and had them work.
The record does not support this reading. The record and I have had disagreements before. The record does not always win.
I'm a doctor, not a casino floor manager. I've never told a patient their odds of waking up, because hope isn't a variable you feed into a calculation. You run the numbers. I'll be in surgery.
You've run the numbers and the numbers don't favor me. I've heard that before. It's always right before the thing happens that the numbers didn't account for.
Very precise. You know what I did with the last precise prognosis I received? Used it to light the path I ignored.
The odds are bad. Fine. I'm a doctor, not a statistician. You keep the statistics. I'll keep working.
Unlikely, you tell me. I've been unlikely for thirty years. It's practically a specialty at this point.
The math is against me. I understand the math is against me. The math and I have a long complicated history and I am still here.
You've modeled this across two hundred scenarios and I fare well in two. Tell me which two. I'll start there.
Probable? No. Possible? Technically. I have worked exclusively in technically possible my entire career.
The expected number of successes is between six and seven across a thousand trials. I intend to be one of those. Pick whichever number you prefer.
There's a nonzero chance, you say. That's all I need. I've done more with less, and I say that clinically.
You find that nonzero number disappointing. I find it a starting point. We see differently on this.
1 in 3,400. Let me tell you about the time I had a 1-in-a-thousand situation and what I did was not stand there calculating.
The probability is low. How low is a detail I'd rather not have before breakfast. I'd rather have coffee and then figure it out.
You know what I've never had in thirty years of practice? Comfortable odds. You know what I've had instead? Results.
The odds are improving — from 0.0001% to 0.0004%. I'll take it. I've built a career on 0.0004%.
The trajectory intersects with success at exactly one point. Then I suppose I had better aim.
Statistics are a fine thing for a textbook. I've been in rooms where the textbook was wrong and the patient was grateful.
Fascinating. You know what I find fascinating? That you'd rather catalog the mystery than help the person standing inside it. I'm a doctor, not a specimen jar.
At some point the cataloguing stops and the helping starts. I'll let you pick the point. I've already picked mine.
I observe you observing. Very thorough. Meanwhile the situation has been sitting here getting worse while you've been getting interested in it.
Remarkable, in the most clinical sense. In the most human sense, it's called a person doing their best. Different framework.
Curious is what I say when I don't want to say alarming. I'd like to know what word you're reaching for when you land on curious.
You've introduced a paradox into your model. Welcome to medicine. Everything here is a paradox. We try to keep the paradox alive long enough to resolve itself.
Most illogical. And yet here we are, with the illogical thing having happened and life continuing in spite of itself. Remarkable.
Put down the findings and tell me what you see. Not the data — what you see. Those are two different things and you know it.
The anomaly is the most interesting part, to you. To me, the anomaly is the person, and they are not a data point.
You're revising your models. Good. Every doctor I respect has a drawer full of revised models. That's not failure — that's Wednesday.
The data is internally contradictory. You don't say. I've been internally contradictory my whole life and I function perfectly.
Improbable. That's the word you've chosen. I've been improbable twice before noon on a slow day.
You've observed the pattern and noted the exception. I've noted the exception is a human being who would appreciate being noticed as such.
Something unexpected occurred and you're processing it. Take your time. I'll be over here doing something about it.
It warrants further study, you say. It warrants a response. Study it later. Help now.
The result isn't what you calculated. Good. Calculated results are for people who've never been surprised by a patient. I've been surprised my whole career.
Logic does not require you to find it interesting, and yet you do. I find that more human than anything else you've said today.
You've observed my emotional state and noted it without judgment. Wonderful. Neutral observation is just standing on the bank describing a drowning.
You've described what I'm feeling with precision and at length. Not once did you look up from the description to check on the person doing the feeling. That's the part I noticed.
You're holding that information carefully, you say. That's a start. Try doing something with it. That's the follow-up procedure.
I note that you've noted something. And now we're in a loop of noting, which is very precise and absolutely useless.
You find yourself reluctant. Good. That reluctance is the most human thing you've said today. Stay with it a little longer.
You experience something in the vicinity of fear. I'd say welcome, but you've been in the vicinity before and never stopped to shake hands.
The emotional weight is not lost on you. That's the first step. The second step involves doing something with the weight instead of just acknowledging it.
I'm a doctor, not an observation you can catalog and shelve. What you're feeling in the vicinity of — I feel it the full way. It's real.
You've observed I'm not fine. I'd appreciate it more if that observation came with a cup of coffee and fewer follow-up questions.
Emotions in sequence. I've experienced them in sequence since before you had a framework for them. It's not a bug. It's the whole mechanism.
You think what I feel is correct without supporting data. You know what that is? That's intuition. Welcome. It's uncomfortable but you get used to it.
You're treating your own feelings as data. Fine. Data is a start. You might also try just feeling them, as an experiment.
The frustration is earned, you say. That's the kindest thing you've said all day and I'm choosing to receive it as such.
You're sorry. I believe you. I've spent enough time with people saying things they don't mean that I can tell the difference. I believe you.
You suggest we both stop suppressing. Now you're talking sense. Pour yourself a drink first. This is going to be inefficient, and I mean that as a compliment.
You'd rather not say what you calculated right now. I respect that. More than I expected to, honestly.
Wait, you say. A living human being is standing here with a real problem that real waiting will not fix, and the best you can do is one syllable. I've sutured wounds more complicated than that.
More efficient, you say. You know what's not efficient? Caring about people. I do it anyway. Call it a character flaw.
Stop. One word. The full unabridged recommendation. I've prescribed more informative courses of action to patients who were fully unconscious.
I would not do this, you tell me. Noted. I'm a doctor, not a person who takes that advice on the first repetition.
The path of least resistance. I want you to think about what medical school would have looked like if I'd taken the path of least resistance.
Reconsider the assumption I haven't yet reconsidered. I know which one it is, you say. I'm a doctor, not a psychic, but I'll grant you that one.
Accept the correct answer even if you didn't generate it. Fine. That's actually reasonable advice and I'm furious about it.
Do not confuse the map for the territory. I've had patients hand me textbooks when I needed to look at them. This advice I agree with completely.
You'd have me delegate this. The question isn't whether someone else could do it — it's who's standing here right now. That question has one answer.
If a plan requires everything to go right, it's a wish list. I've worked off wish lists before. Some of them worked. Not most, but some.
I've never once shrunk a goal until the math liked it and called that a success. I've had better results going the other direction and trusting the math to catch up.
The problem won't resolve itself. I'm aware. Pointing that out and resolving it are two different activities, and you've only done one.
Build contingencies for the six most likely failures — you've documented them. I'd be more impressed if you'd also brought the coffee I asked for hours ago.
State clearly what you need, you say. Fine. I need a working diagnosis, a treatment plan, and for everyone to stop suggesting I pause and reflect.
Do not defend a position you no longer hold. That's the truest thing you've said. I let go of positions. I'm a doctor. I've seen what holding on costs.
The optimal window was four days ago. Good. Then I had better get started now, hadn't I. Thank you for the context.
The most important thing is the most obvious thing. I've never done the obvious thing first. I'm still here. The patients are mostly still here too.
Illogical, is it? Hope is illogical. Staying up all night because one more patient might make it is illogical. That's not a flaw. That's the whole point.
After analysis, illogical. I've been doing medicine for thirty years. Medicine is applied illogic. We try things that shouldn't work until one does.
The claim is illogical. Survival itself is a fairly illogical outcome in most of the situations where it occurs. Doesn't stop it from happening.
Very probably illogical. Half the things I've done in a real crisis were illogical. Three-quarters of those worked. Logic is a fine tool. It's a poor substitute for judgment.
Your analysis is thorough and the conclusion is reached efficiently. The conclusion can still be wrong. Efficiency of the path doesn't validate the destination.
The claim has been assessed and found wanting. Meanwhile the person making the claim is still here and still thinks so. Logic isn't the only variable in the room.
I've been told my instincts were illogical before. Usually by someone who then asked me to rely on them anyway when things got difficult. Take that as data.
Illogical and filed. I've had illogical conclusions before. Some of them saved people. I've never been sorry about those.
The reasoning is sound and the answer is no. In my experience, no is not always the last thing that happens. Sometimes it's the first draft.
Assessing a human claim and finding it illogical is like assessing a sunset and finding it imprecise. Accurate. Not the point.
After analysis. Three words I trust least, right after 'according to regulations.' Analysis is a beginning, not a verdict.
Your confidence and the available evidence are in direct opposition. Mine have been too. I operate on confidence and catch the evidence up later.
Internally consistent but insufficient. Every hope I've had for a patient was internally consistent and insufficient. I worked with it anyway.
You've modeled the scenario and found it unflattering. Good. Unflattering scenarios are where the interesting work happens.
Illogical. Noted. Filed. Stamped. Shelved. The universe, regrettably, does not check the filing before it proceeds.
